title: [rlsw] `LoadRenderTexture()` framebuffer is `INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT` unless `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE == D32`
author: HurricanVD
body:
### Issue description

With the software renderer (`GRAPHICS_API_OPENGL_SOFTWARE`), `LoadRenderTexture()` produces an **incomplete framebuffer** whenever `rlsw` is compiled with a depth type other than the default `D32` (i.e. `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE = D8` or `D16`). `rlFramebufferComplete()` logs `WARNING: FBO: [ID 1] Framebuffer has incomplete attachment` and any `BeginTextureMode()`/`EndTextureMode()` then renders into nothing (the render texture stays empty). The default `D32` build works, so the bug is latent and only appears once you pick a smaller depth buffer (e.g. to save memory on a constrained target).

### Root cause

The `rlsw` rasterizer is bound at **compile time** to exactly one depth format, `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_FORMAT` (from `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE`); depth is read/written via the compile-time `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_GET/SET` macros with a fixed stride. So every depth attachment must have that format, and `swCheckFramebufferStatus()` enforces it:

```c
// external/rlsw.h - swCheckFramebufferStatus()
if (RLSW.depthBuffer->format != SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_FORMAT)
    return SW_FRAMEBUFFER_INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT;
```

But the FBO depth attachment is created with a fixed format that ignores the compiled type, at two spots:

**1. `src/rlgl.h`, `rlLoadTextureDepth()` software branch** hard-codes `GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT32` (its own comment even warns about it):

```c
#elif defined(GRAPHICS_API_OPENGL_SOFTWARE)
    // NOTE: Renderbuffers are the same type of object as textures in rlsw
    // WARNING: Ensure that the depth format is the one specified at rlsw compilation
    glGenRenderbuffers(1, &id);
    glBindRenderbuffer(GL_RENDERBUFFER, id);
    glRenderbufferStorage(GL_RENDERBUFFER, GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT32, width, height); // hard-coded
    glBindRenderbuffer(GL_RENDERBUFFER, 0);
#endif
```

**2. `src/external/rlsw.h`, `swTexStorage2D()`** maps every depth internal format to `D16`/`D32` regardless of the compiled type (there is no `GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT8` enum):

```c
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT16:  pixelFormat = SW_PIXELFORMAT_DEPTH_D16; break;
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT24:  pixelFormat = SW_PIXELFORMAT_DEPTH_D32; break;
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT32:  pixelFormat = SW_PIXELFORMAT_DEPTH_D32; break;
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT32F: pixelFormat = SW_PIXELFORMAT_DEPTH_D32; break;
```

On a `D8` build the FBO depth attachment ends up `D32`, so `D32 != D8` -> `INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT`. On the default `D32` build the two happen to agree, which is why the default build works.

### Steps to reproduce

Build `rlsw` with a non-default depth type and create a render texture:

```c
// rlsw compiled with -DSW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE=D8  (or D16)
InitWindow(800, 450, "rt");
RenderTexture2D t = LoadRenderTexture(160, 90);
// console: "WARNING: FBO: [ID 1] Framebuffer has incomplete attachment"
```

The stock `examples/core/core_smooth_pixelperfect` then shows an empty canvas (text overlays draw fine; the up-scaled render-texture region stays blank).

**Minimal engine-free reproduction (runs on any host).** `rlsw` is pure software, so the completeness path can be driven without a window/GL: `#define RLSW_IMPLEMENTATION`, `swInit()`, then create a color texture + a depth renderbuffer via `glRenderbufferStorage(GL_RENDERBUFFER, GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT32, ...)`, attach both, and print `glCheckFramebufferStatus()` - i.e. exactly the `gl* -> sw*` calls `rlgl` makes. Built with native x86_64 mingw-gcc against the unmodified upstream `rlsw.h`, changing only `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE`:

| SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE | framebuffer status |
|---|---|
| `D8`  | `0x8cd6` INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT |
| `D16` | `0x8cd6` INCOMPLETE_ATTACHMENT |
| `D32` (default) | `0x8cd5` COMPLETE |

### Proposed fix

Any depth attachment used by the rasterizer must have the one compile-time format (`SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_FORMAT`) - depth uses the fixed-stride `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_GET/SET` macros, so a different-format depth buffer would also be *misread*, not merely incomplete. Two ways:

**Option A - minimal (rlsw only).** Coerce any depth request in `swTexStorage2D()` to the compiled format:

```c
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT16:
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT24:
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT32:
case SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT32F: pixelFormat = SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_FORMAT; break;
```

One line, one file, covers `D8` (no GL enum exists for it); default `D32` unchanged.

**Option B - explicit format (rlsw + rlgl).** If you prefer the format *named* rather than silently coerced: add a (non-standard) `SW_DEPTH_COMPONENT8` to `SWinternalformat` (the internal `SW_PIXELFORMAT_DEPTH_D8` already exists) and map it in `swTexStorage2D()`; then in `rlgl.h` `rlLoadTextureDepth()` (software) replace the hard-coded `GL_DEPTH_COMPONENT32` with the enum matching the compiled `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE`. More faithful, but touches two files, needs a non-standard enum, and adds no capability (the rasterizer is single-format, so `rlLoadTextureDepth` must request exactly the compiled format anyway).

Either way, keep the `depthBuffer->format != SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_FORMAT` check in `swCheckFramebufferStatus()` as the backstop. (I used Option A in my port and verified it - table above.)

### Environment

- raylib 6.1-dev @ `f00317be`
- `GRAPHICS_API_OPENGL_SOFTWARE` (rlsw), `SW_FRAMEBUFFER_DEPTH_TYPE = D8`
- Found while porting raylib to AmigaOS 3.2 (68k, software renderer); the minimal repro above was run and confirmed on Windows x86_64 (mingw-gcc 15.2) against the unmodified upstream `rlsw.h`.

title: TextSubtext() returns an empty string for position == 0 (breaks the text_writing_anim example)
author: HurricanVD
body:
**Module:** rtext
**raylib version:** master (`f00317be`, `6.0-218`); also present on current HEAD
**Platform/compiler:** platform-independent (logic bug in `rtext.c`; found while porting to an AmigaOS software renderer, but the code path is not platform-specific)

### Description

`TextSubtext(text, position, length)` guards its copy with `(position > 0)`, so a call with `position == 0` (the start of the string) falls through and returns an empty string. Position 0 is a valid start offset, and it is exactly what raylib's own example `examples/text/text_writing_anim.c` uses:

```c
DrawText(TextSubtext(message, 0, framesCounter/10), 210, 160, 20, MAROON);
```

As a result, that example's typewriter reveal never draws anything — only the two static hint lines appear. Any code that reveals a string from its beginning via `TextSubtext(s, 0, n)` is affected.

### Root cause

`src/rtext.c`, `TextSubtext()`:

```c
if ((text != NULL) && (position > 0) && (length > 0))   // position > 0 drops the valid position 0
```

The `(position > 0)` guard was introduced in the recent text-API hardening series. Before that, the function only checked `(text != NULL)` and handled position 0 correctly.

### Fix

```diff
-    if ((text != NULL) && (position > 0) && (length > 0))
+    if ((text != NULL) && (position >= 0) && (length > 0))
```

`(length > 0)` is fine (length 0 -> empty substring is correct); the inner `if (position < textLength)` already bounds the upper end. Only the lower bound needs to include 0.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Build and run `examples/text/text_writing_anim`.
2. Expected: the MAROON message is revealed character by character.
3. Actual: the MAROON message never appears (empty substring); only the two hint lines render.

Minimal check:

```c
const char *s = TextSubtext("Hello", 0, 3);   // expected "Hel", actual "" on master
```

title: [rcore] Static raylib.lib does not initialize window on AMD A6-3420M
author: JstnJrg

title: [rlgl] change mediump to highp precision for Android to prevent shimmering/aliasing
author: meowstr
body:
### Issue description

The default shaders for OpenGL ES 2.0:
https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/blob/caadb48e259028233515777a3e6402040c497309/src/rlgl.h#L5018
specifies `mediump` for the float precision, however on phones this actually means 16-bit precision and with pixel coordinates in the thousands, this precision is much to small to accurately compute triangle vertices in screen coordinates. Its only 10 mantissa digits and my phone screen is 1080x2424. This causes nasty shimmering/aliasing effects as shapes moves towards the bottom right of the screen. Changing the shaders from `mediump` to `highp` fixes this issue completely. Could we have some sort of setting for this or perhaps default to `highp` for everything? 

It seems that desktop platforms commonly interpret `mediump` as 32 bit which makes this problem not happen on desktop.

### Environment

On Android via raymob template.

title: [examples] Possible bug in `pbr.fs`
author: ruifig
body:
In all 3 pbr.fs shaders, I believe this line has a bug:
```
N = texture(normalMap, vec2(fragTexCoord.x*tiling.x + offset.y, fragTexCoord.y*tiling.y + offset.y)).rgb;
```

I'm a graphics rookie, but `fragTexCoord.x*tiling.x + offset.y` should probably be `fragTexCoord.x*tiling.x + offset.x` ?

title: [rcore] `SetMousePosition()` doesn't update `GetMouseDelta()`
author: bartimaeusnek

title: [rlsw] Persistent translucent current color loses blending after first primitive
author: HurricanVD
body:
**Affected:** `external/rlsw.h` v1.5 (`GRAPHICS_API_OPENGL_SOFTWARE`).
Verified against raylib master `caadb48e`; also present in `65abee1c`.
Platform-independent - reproduced on x86 (Windows/MinGW, both SSE and x87 FP)
and on m68k/AmigaOS.

## Summary

In rlsw immediate mode, a translucent current color set once before multiple
primitives is only considered for the first completed primitive. After the
primitive flush, `hasColorAlpha` is reset even though the current color
persists, so the blend-elision path disables `SW_STATE_BLEND` for later
untextured primitives.

## Minimal reproduction (pure rlsw)

```c
swEnable(SW_BLEND); swBlendFunc(SW_SRC_ALPHA, SW_ONE_MINUS_SRC_ALPHA);
// framebuffer cleared to white

swBegin(SW_TRIANGLES);
swColor4ub(135, 60, 190, 153);      // set ONCE, alpha ~0.6 (GL current color)
swVertex2f(100,100); swVertex2f(100,200); swVertex2f(200,100);  // triangle 1
swVertex2f(200,100); swVertex2f(100,200); swVertex2f(200,200);  // triangle 2
swEnd();
```

**Expected:** both triangles blend identically (~60% violet over white).
**Actual:** triangle 1 is blended; triangle 2 renders unblended/source-like
(pure vertex color, as if `SW_BLEND` were off). Illustrative readback from my
harness: triangle 1 interior ~= (183,138,216), triangle 2 interior = (135,60,190)
(exact byte order depends on the configured framebuffer output format; the
qualitative "first blended, second source-like" split is the point).

## Reproduction at raylib level

With `SUPPORT_QUADS_DRAW_MODE=0`, `DrawRectangleRec(rec, Fade(VIOLET, 0.6f))`
emits one `rlColor4ub` followed by two triangles: the result shows a hard
diagonal seam (first triangle blended, second opaque).
`DrawRectangleLinesEx(rec, 6, Fade(BLACK, 0.3f))` renders partly opaque black
borders. This is how we noticed (raylib's `shapes_colors_palette` example,
hover state).

Note on why the default desktop config rarely shows it: with
`SUPPORT_QUADS_DRAW_MODE=1` most shapes bind the shapes/font texture, whose
GRAY_ALPHA format keeps the `boundTexture->alpha != SW_PIXEL_ALPHA_NONE`
escape in the elision check alive, so blending survives. Functions that
re-submit the color per primitive (e.g. `DrawPoly`, which calls `rlColor4ub`
per segment) are also unaffected. The bug needs an untextured path with a
single persistent translucent color - which is standard GL immediate-mode
usage.

## Root cause

`RLSW.primitive.hasColorAlpha` is used as the basis for the blend-elision
optimization in `sw_immediate_push_vertex()`:

```c
else if ((RLSW.blendFlags & SW_BLEND_FLAG_NEEDS_ALPHA) && (!RLSW.primitive.hasColorAlpha))
    ... state &= ~SW_STATE_BLEND;
```

The flag is OR-ed in by `sw_immediate_set_color()`, but it does not conserve
the persistent current color across primitive boundaries: it is hard-reset to
`false` both in `sw_immediate_begin()` and after each primitive flush in
`sw_immediate_push_vertex()`. Since the current color persists across
primitives and across `swEnd()` (GL semantics) and seeds every following
vertex, all primitives after the first lose `SW_STATE_BLEND` unless the
application happens to re-submit the color.

## Suggested fix

Re-derive the flag from the persisting current color instead of clearing it,
at both reset sites:

```c
// sw_immediate_begin(), and after the primitive flush in sw_immediate_push_vertex():
RLSW.primitive.hasColorAlpha = (RLSW.primitive.color[3] < 1.0f);
```

The `|=` in `sw_immediate_set_color()` stays as is, so a translucent color
submitted mid-primitive still marks the whole primitive.

Verified with this change: the two-triangle rect is pixel-uniform, the
`DrawRectangleLinesEx` borders come out at the exact 30% blend, and a
regression sweep over the plain / blend / depth / blend+depth raster paths
shows no behavior change for opaque colors.

Found while porting raylib to AmigaOS 3.x (m68k, rlsw backend); first
suspected our cross-compiler, but an x86 host harness with vanilla rlsw
reproduced it 1:1.

title: [rlsw] SW_LOG always prints to stdout
author: ghaberek

title: [rcore] Raylib 6.0 window freezing (only from VCPKG)
author: cos1110
body:
The library works correctly if built from source, however if downloaded from VCPKG, the problem is that the window will freeze and would be unable to process events, during main loop. This happens during the call of `EndDrawing` function.

Based on some examination, I used the debug symbols from git-source-build, and then I linked to VCPKG lib path, and managed to figure out that the `SUPPORT_CUSTOM_FRAME_CONTROL` flag isn't taken into consideration.

However then linking to git-source-build it appears that the flag is defined properly.

Video that shows the behavior:
https://mega.nz/file/xTkHFSIB#_mgz_0cucMEuos8q31a8mCenLlqdpCf12XwIf3HMdWk

title: [rmodels] Build failure in 'LoadIQM' on GCC 16.1
author: eloj
body:
### Issue description

The IQM object loader code is assigning an `unsigned int` from `iqmHeader->num_joints` to `boneCount` in `struct ModelSkeleton`, which is an `int`. The compiler detects that this can potentially overflow into a negative value, which is then passed to the allocator as a `size_t` potentially resulting in a huge allocation.

Tested at bb96b920d34eed6

### The Error

```shell
[ 36%] Linking C executable models_orthographic_projection
In function 'LoadIQM',
    inlined from 'LoadModel.constprop' at /home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:1110:52:
/home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:5014:38: error: argument 1 range [18446744071562067968, 18446744073709551615] exceeds maximum object size 9223372036854775807 [-Werror=alloc-size-larger-than=]
 5014 |     model.currentPose = (Transform *)RL_CALLOC(model.skeleton.boneCount, sizeof(Transform));
      |                                      ^
/usr/include/stdlib.h: In function 'LoadModel.constprop':
/usr/include/stdlib.h:675:14: note: in a call to allocation function 'calloc' declared here
  675 | extern void *calloc (size_t __nmemb, size_t __size)
      |              ^
In function 'LoadIQM',
    inlined from 'LoadModel.constprop' at /home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:1110:52:
/home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:5015:36: error: argument 1 range [18446744071562067968, 18446744073709551615] exceeds maximum object size 9223372036854775807 [-Werror=alloc-size-larger-than=]
 5015 |     model.boneMatrices = (Matrix *)RL_CALLOC(model.skeleton.boneCount, sizeof(Matrix));
      |                                    ^
/usr/include/stdlib.h: In function 'LoadModel.constprop':
/usr/include/stdlib.h:675:14: note: in a call to allocation function 'calloc' declared here
  675 | extern void *calloc (size_t __nmemb, size_t __size)
      |              ^
In function 'LoadIQM',
    inlined from 'LoadModel.constprop' at /home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:1110:52:
/home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:5014:38: error: argument 1 range [18446744071562067968, 18446744073709551615] exceeds maximum object size 9223372036854775807 [-Werror=alloc-size-larger-than=]
 5014 |     model.currentPose = (Transform *)RL_CALLOC(model.skeleton.boneCount, sizeof(Transform));
      |                                      ^
/usr/include/stdlib.h: In function 'LoadModel.constprop':
/usr/include/stdlib.h:675:14: note: in a call to allocation function 'calloc' declared here
  675 | extern void *calloc (size_t __nmemb, size_t __size)
      |              ^
In function 'LoadIQM',
    inlined from 'LoadModel.constprop' at /home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:1110:52:
/home/eddy/dev/EXT/raylib/src/rmodels.c:5015:36: error: argument 1 range [18446744071562067968, 18446744073709551615] exceeds maximum object size 9223372036854775807 [-Werror=alloc-size-larger-than=]
 5015 |     model.boneMatrices = (Matrix *)RL_CALLOC(model.skeleton.boneCount, sizeof(Matrix));
      |                                    ^
/usr/include/stdlib.h: In function 'LoadModel.constprop':
/usr/include/stdlib.h:675:14: note: in a call to allocation function 'calloc' declared here
  675 | extern void *calloc (size_t __nmemb, size_t __size)
      |              ^
lto1: all warnings being treated as errors
make[3]: *** [/tmp/ccsbdewr.mk:14: /tmp/cc7nxShc.ltrans6.ltrans.o] Error 1
```

### Build

This is my build command:

```bash
EXTRA_CFLAGS="-flto=auto -fuse-linker-plugin"
cmake -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS="-march=native -mtune=native -Werror ${EXTRA_CFLAGS}" -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=${PREFIX} \
	-DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RelWithDebInfo \
	-DUSE_EXTERNAL_GLFW=ON -DGLFW_BUILD_WAYLAND=OFF \
	-S $HOME/dev/EXT/raylib -B build
```

### The fix

Proposed minimum fix:

```diff
diff --git a/src/rmodels.c b/src/rmodels.c
index 28fb3c9ec..bde056186 100644
--- a/src/rmodels.c
+++ b/src/rmodels.c
@@ -4981,7 +4981,7 @@ static Model LoadIQM(const char *fileName)
     //fread(ijoint, sizeof(IQMJoint), iqmHeader->num_joints, iqmFile);
     memcpy(ijoint, fileDataPtr + iqmHeader->ofs_joints, iqmHeader->num_joints*sizeof(IQMJoint));
 
-    model.skeleton.boneCount = iqmHeader->num_joints;
+    model.skeleton.boneCount = iqmHeader->num_joints & INT_MAX;
     model.skeleton.bones = (BoneInfo *)RL_CALLOC(iqmHeader->num_joints, sizeof(BoneInfo));
     model.skeleton.bindPose = (Transform *)RL_CALLOC(iqmHeader->num_joints, sizeof(Transform));
```

Obviously a more holistic fix would also adjust the local allocations to use the capped value, but I doubt any real/non-adversarial model would ever be affected by the mask to begin with.

### Workaround

Setting `SUPPORT_FILEFORMAT_IQM` to zero in the configuration allows the build to proceed successfully.

title: [Docs] FreeBSD build instructions: wrong compiler and missing "-lm"
author: MilkGolium
body:
**Problem**
The FreeBSD section of the documentation has two issues:
1. It suggests using `gcc` as the compiler, but FreeBSD's default compiler is `cc` (clang).
2. The compile command is missing the `-lm` flag, which causes linker errors when building examples.

**Steps to Reproduce**
1. Install raylib via `pkg install raylib` on FreeBSD.
2. Follow the documented build command using `gcc`.
3. Compilation fails: headers not found, and linker errors due to missing `-lm`.

```text
voosk@V_FBServer ~/clang $ gcc core_basic_window.c -o raylib -lraylib -lm
voosk@V_FBServer ~/clang $ 
voosk@V_FBServer ~/clang $ gcc core_basic_window.c -o raylib -lraylib    
/usr/local/bin/ld: /tmp//ccttgjoo.o: undefined reference to symbol 'fmaxf@@FBSD_1.0'
/usr/local/bin/ld: /lib/libm.so.5: error adding symbols: DSO missing from command line
collect2: error: ld returned 1 exit status
voosk@V_FBServer ~/clang $
voosk@V_FBServer ~/clang $ uname -a         
FreeBSD V_FBServer 15.1-RELEASE-p1 FreeBSD 15.1-RELEASE-p1 releng/15.1-n283582-0f691888dc56 GENERIC amd64
```

**Expected**
The documentation should reflect FreeBSD's default compiler (`cc`) and include the required `-lm` flag.

**Suggested Fix**
Update the FreeBSD build instructions to:

```sh
cc core_basic_window.c -o raylib -I/usr/local/include -L/usr/local/lib -lraylib
```

title: [rcore] Outdated joystick API's for SDL3 backend
author: 0xFishbyte
body:
**WARNING: Please, read this note carefully before submitting a new issue:**

It is important to realise that **this is NOT A SUPPORT FORUM**, this is for reproducible BUGS with raylib ONLY.

There are lots of generous and helpful people ready to help you out on [raylib Discord forum](https://discord.gg/raylib) or [raylib reddit](https://www.reddit.com/r/raylib/).

Remember that asking for support questions here actively takes developer time away from improving raylib.

---

Please, before submitting a new issue verify and check:

 - [x] I tested it on latest raylib version from master branch
 - [x] I checked there is no similar issue already reported
 - [x] I checked the documentation on the [wiki](https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/wiki)
 - [x] My code has no errors or misuse of raylib

### Issue description

**RCORE** is using old SDL2 API's for joystick initialization stuff even when building for SDL3. This already caused issues with gamepad detection at least on my machine:

```
WARNING: PLATFORM: Unable to open game controller [ERROR: Parameter 'joystick' is invalid]
INFO: PLATFORM: DESKTOP (SDL3): Initialized successfully
```
The culprit is:
https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/blob/a7ac5c2823d767002ca644b24708353c628cad08/src/platforms/rcore_desktop_sdl.c#L2167-L2172

But according to [SDL migration guide](https://wiki.libsdl.org/SDL3/README-migration):

```
SDL_JoystickInstanceID() => SDL_GetJoystickID()
```
```
SDL_GameControllerGetJoystick() => SDL_GetGamepadJoystick()
```
and
```
SDL_GameControllerOpen() => SDL_OpenGamepad()
```

### Environment

*Provide your Platform, Operating System, OpenGL version, GPU details where you experienced the issue.*
SDL3, Linux (CachyOS), OpenGL 4.6, GPU is irrelevant i guess but controller was [Thrustmaster T.FLIGHT STICK X](https://shop.thrustmaster.com/nl_nl/t-flight-stick-x.html) 

**Would you like a PR to fix this?**

title: [rcore] When mouse is outside window Raylib gives stale mouse information
author: largenumberhere
body: > Please, before submitting a new issue verify and check: > - [x] I tested it on latest raylib version from master branch > - [x] I checked there is no similar issue already reported > - [x] I checked the documentation on the [wiki](https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/wiki) > - [x] My code has no errors or misuse of raylib ### Issue description #### Background I've been messing around making my own wrappers for raylib drawing logic lately. I have a window where I want to detect which section of the window is hovered over and respond to it correctly. I noticed through this process that raylib does not seem to clearly report when I move the mouse outside of the window. At first I assumed `GetMousePosition` was returning some default value, and adjusted my logic accordingly and still had strange behaviour. #### Details When moving the mouse cursor outside of the window, `GetMousePosition` returns stale values, and no other window function seems to notice (`IsWindowState`, `IsWindowFocused`, etc). In particular, `GetMousePosition` returns a value somewhat close to where the mouse exited the window. How accurate it is, depends on how fast I move the mouse out of the window. This makes it wholly un-useful information. #### Proposal For a simple solution, I think that `GetMousePosition` should return `0,0` when the cursor is outside the window. It is extremely unlikely that any user will move their cursor to exactly this position. It is also sane default that can be ignored when not important, and has an intuitive meaning of initialized but probably not meaningful. It is also internally consistent because it matches the default cursor position when the window has been launched but not yet hovered over. As for implementation, as starting point glfw has [cursor enter/leave callbacks](https://www.glfw.org/docs/3.3/input_guide.html#cursor_enter). They can be used to observe these events and update state without much hassle. My intuition says that `IsWindowFocused()` should also handle this case by returning false, even if the window manager would still technically classify the window as focused. This however, depends on the intended precise semantics of what 'Focused' should mean in raylib. ### Environment Os: `Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS distribution.` Raylib: `6.0.0` OpenGl: ``` INFO: GL: OpenGL device information: INFO: > Vendor: Intel INFO: > Renderer: Mesa Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (ADL GT2) INFO: > Version: 4.6 (Core Profile) Mesa 25.2.8-0ubuntu0.24.04.2 INFO: > GLSL: 4.60 ``` Gpu ``` *-display description: 3D controller product: GA106M [GeForce RTX 3060 Mobile / Max-Q] vendor: NVIDIA Corporation *-display product: Alder Lake-P GT2 [Iris Xe Graphics] ``` ### Issue Screenshot **The cursor is outside the window** but the values do not indicate it. The IsWindowFocused function is affected by my screenshot software, it is actually reporting true. ### Code Example ```c // watch what happens when you move your cursor off the edge of the window. #include "raylib.h" int main() { InitWindow(500, 500, "mouse position demo"); while (!WindowShouldClose()) { BeginDrawing(); ClearBackground(RAYWHITE); char buffer [512] = {0}; Vector2 pos = GetMousePosition(); bool focused = IsWindowFocused(); sprintf(buffer, "cursor at %10.1f, %10.1f.\nFocused?: %i\n", pos.x, pos.y, focused); DrawText(buffer, 0,0, 30, RED); EndDrawing(); } return 0; } ```

title: [rtext/etc.] `DrawText(int x, y)`/`DrawTextAlts...(Vector2 position)` Conflates `int` With `float`
author: 8e8m

title: [rcore] expose waking event waiting
author: HookedBehemoth

title: [rcore] `GetWindowPosition()` incorrect after `MaximizeWindow()`
author: rossberg
body:
When calling GetWindowPosition() after MaximizeWindow(), it no longer reports the correct position, but the one from before maximizing. (Using GLFW on MacOS)

This used to work correctly in Raylib 5.5. I suspect it broke with https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/commit/890ca8d6870585f9e3499eb0e7a327af2cabaa1b, which changed GetWindowPosition to return the buffered size, but without adapting Maximize/Minimize/RestoreWindow to update that size.

title: [build][Zig] Support for upcoming `Zig 0.17`
author: Yinameah
body:
### Issue description

zig 0.17 (not released yet) breaks the build system a bit ( for the better of course 😉 ).

I've been descending DVUI (gui library) dependency tree to test the new incremental compilation capabilities of zig, and this lead me to fork and update quite some packages.

So this issue is merely [to share it here](https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/compare/master...Yinameah:raylib:0.17) hoping it will make someone's live easier in the future.

Note that there is two commits. First one is to catch up with `zemscripten` latest commit that changed a bit it's API and the second to support 0.17 (not backward compatible. It probably could, but would require `zemscripten` to do the same).

Note as well that I changed `zemscripten` url to point to my (temporary) fork and that I tested the build only on linux. 

All the best.

title: [build][CMake] Linker errors when building raylib as static library and using OpenGL ES 2.0
author: FattiMei
body:
- [x] I tested it on latest raylib version from master branch
 - [] I checked there is no similar issue already reported: maybe #4648
 - [ ] I checked the documentation on the [wiki](https://github.com/raysan5/raylib/wiki)
 - [x] My code has no errors or misuse of raylib: no code needed

### Issue description
I'm trying to compile raylib with the pair `PLATFORM=Desktop` and `OPENGL_VERSION="ES 2.0"`. I know from discussions in #4648 that this is not a priority, but this issue exposes unexpected behaviour in the cmake build system.

If I configure raylib to be a static library, with `PLATFORM=Desktop` and `OPENGL_VERSION="ES 2.0"`, I install it and load it from an external cmake project with `find_package(raylib)` I get linker errors describing missing opengl symbols like `glClear`

If I configure raylib to be a shared library instead, the linker errors are solved and the build completes.

### Environment
This has been tested on linux machines:
  * Raspberry PI 1B+ (armv6l)
  * Radxa (aarch64) - Debian bookworm
  * x86 machine - Ubuntu 24.04 LTS
I will explain why I don't think this is an environment problem

### Code Example
To reproduce the build, we will use the provided CMake project

Static library build, fails with linker errors for unresolved opengl symbols
```cmake
cd raylib
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug -DBUILD_EXAMPLES:BOOL=OFF -DPLATFORM=Desktop -DOPENGL_VERSION="ES 2.0"
make -j
sudo make install

cd ../projects/CMake
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DPLATFORM=Desktop
make
```

Dynamic library build, all good
```cmake
cd raylib
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug -DBUILD_EXAMPLES:BOOL=OFF -DPLATFORM=Desktop -DOPENGL_VERSION="ES 2.0" -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS:BOOL=ON"
make -j
sudo make uninstall # this is important for reprod
sudo make install

cd ../projects/CMake
mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. -DPLATFORM=Desktop
make
```

There is a catch because the installation may not rewrite some files, so it's better to uninstall the previous version before installing raylib once again

### Solution
I have come up with a number of solution in the static library case, maybe this issue can be a reference for other users.

One can import the raylib package and at the moment of linking just add the libraries needed
```cmake
set(RAYLIB_VERSION 6.0)
find_package(raylib ${RAYLIB_VERSION} REQUIRED)

add_executable(main core_basic_window.c)
target_link_libraries(${PROJECT_NAME} raylib GL) # or whatever the user wants, maybe it's more proper to use GLESv2
```

but this solution goes against the spirit of a cmake build. The user should not be thinking about the dependencies of raylib.

The other solution involves modifying the cmake build system to:
  1. properly include the opengl es2 packages (and maybe we can also use the occasion to work on supporting opengl es3)
  2. change the way in which dependencies are exported

for (2) I have found that the file `src/CMakeLists.txt` at line 107 says
```cmake
target_link_libraries(raylib PRIVATE $<BUILD_INTERFACE:${LIBS_PRIVATE}>)
```

changing it to
```cmake
target_link_libraries(raylib PRIVATE ${LIBS_PRIVATE})
```

solves the problem for me

### Elephant in the room
I'm trying to use ES2.0, but I'm linking with GL. Yes, I have also tried to manually add GLESv2 to the `LIBS_PRIVATE` variable in `cmake/LibraryConfigurations.cmake` at line 80, but that didn't solve my problem. If this issue gets picked I can dig more into it

### Conclusions
This issue is not blocking for me, as I have applied duct tape. It could be an enhancement in the cmake experience of the project. I understand the desire to keep the system free from complications.
